docs.title: Welcome to Weni CLI
docs.text: Weni CLI is a command-line tool that simplifies the creation and management of multiple AI agents quickly and efficiently. Integrated with the Weni platform, it enables the development and deployment of high-performance agents across various communication channels, such as WhatsApp, Instagram, Facebook, and more.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
With it, you can:

  • Create AI agents
  • Add custom skills to your agents
  • Deploy agents
  • Update agent configurations and behaviors
  • Manage multiple agents in your projects

docs.title: Contributing to Weni CLI
docs.text: Thank you for your interest in contributing to Weni CLI! This guide will help you get started with contributing to the project.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python >= 3.12
  • Poetry >= 1.8.5
  • Git

docs.title: Setting Up Development Environment
docs.text:
1. Fork the repository on GitHub

  2. Clone your fork: 

    git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/weni-cli.git
cd weni-cli

  3. Install dependencies: 

    poetry install

  4. Create a new branch: 

    git checkout -b feature/your-feature-name

docs.title: Code Style
docs.text:
• Follow PEP 8 guidelines
  • Use meaningful variable and function names
  • Add docstrings to functions and classes
  • Keep functions focused and concise

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
1. Write tests for new features: 

    def test_your_feature():
    # Your test code here
    assert expected == actual

  2. Run tests: 

    poetry run pytest

  3. Check coverage: 

    poetry run pytest --cov

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
1. Update documentation for new features
  2. Add docstrings to new functions
  3. Update README.md if needed
  4. Add examples when relevant

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
1. Create a feature branch
  2. Make your changes
  3. Write or update tests
  4. Update documentation
  5. Run tests locally
  6. Commit your changes
  7. Push to your fork
  8. Create a Pull Request

docs.title: Commit Messages
docs.text:
Follow conventional commits:

  • feat: New features
  • fix: Bug fixes
  • docs: Documentation changes
  • test: Adding or updating tests
  • chore: Maintenance tasks

Example: 

git commit -m "feat: add support for custom headers in requests"

docs.title: PR Guidelines
docs.text:
1. Create one PR per feature/fix
  2. Include tests
  3. Update documentation
  4. Reference issues if applicable
  5. Keep changes focused and minimal

docs.title: PR Template
docs.text:
## Description
Brief description of changes

## Type of Change
- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] New feature
- [ ] Documentation update
- [ ] Other (specify)

## Testing
Describe testing done

## Documentation
List documentation updates

docs.title: Review Process
docs.text:
1. Automated checks must pass
  2. Code review by maintainers
  3. Documentation review
  4. Changes requested if needed
  5. Approval and merge

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
• Create an issue for bugs
  • Ask questions in discussions
  • Join our community channels

docs.title: Recommended VSCode Extensions
docs.text:
• Python
  • YAML
  • markdownlint
  • GitLens

docs.title: Useful Commands
docs.text:
# Format code
poetry run black .

# Run linter
poetry run flake8

# Run tests
poetry run pytest

# Build documentation
poetry run mkdocs serve

docs.title: Release Process
docs.text:
1. Version bump
  2. Update CHANGELOG
  3. Create release PR
  4. Tag release
  5. Deploy to PyPI

docs.title: Community
docs.text:
• Be respectful and inclusive
  • Help others when possible
  • Share knowledge
  • Follow our Code of Conduct

Thank you for contributing to Weni CLI! 🎉

docs.title: Project Commands
docs.text:
These commands are fundamental for developing and deploying agents, allowing direct interaction with your project on the Weni platform. They help you manage your project workflow, from authentication to project selection and management.

Command Description weni login This is how authentication happens. Use it to authenticate according to your Weni platform account. weni project list Existing projects in your account will be listed using this command. weni project use With this command you can choose a specific project to work with and deploy agents. weni project current Use this to identify the project identifier you are currently working with. weni project push This command allows you to deploy/update your agents.

docs.title: Community
docs.text: Engage with the Weni community

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Agents are AI-powered workers designed to operate autonomously within specific contexts, using generative AI to make decisions based on given problems. In the context of Weni CLI, agents are specifically optimized for customer service operations, serving as the frontline communication interface between companies and their customers.

Key features:

  • Autonomous decision-making capabilities
  • Context-specific operations
  • Built-in generative AI processing
  • Customer service optimization

With Weni CLI, you can define and deploy multiple agents that work together to solve real-world problems with precision, quality, and security. These agents can be equipped with various skills that enable them to interact with the external world within defined boundaries.

docs.title: Creating an Agent
docs.text:
An agent consists of an agent definition. In Weni CLI, this definition is made using a YAML file, where you can write your agent however you want.

Here is an example of how you can define your agent in a YAML file:

agent_definition.yaml
agents:
  sample_agent:
    name: "Sample Agent"
    description: "Weni's sample agent"
    instructions:
      - "You should always be polite, respectful and helpful, even if the user is not."
      - "If you don't know the answer, don't lie. Tell the user you don't know."
    guardrails:
      - "Don't talk about politics, religion or any other sensitive topic. Keep it neutral."
    skills:
      - get_order_status:
          name: "Get Order Status"
          source: 
            path: "skills/order_status"
            entrypoint: "lambda_function.lambda_handler"
          description: "Function to get the order status"
          parameters:
            - order_id:
                description: "Order ID"
                type: "string"
                required: true
      - get_order_details:
          name: "Get Order Details"                                                           
          source: 
            path: "skills/order_details"
            entrypoint: "lambda_function.lambda_handler"
          description: "Function to get the order details"
          parameters:
            - order_id:
                description: "Order ID"
                type: "string"
                required: true

docs.title: YAML Elements
docs.text:
AgentSkill Name

The name of your agent that will be displayed in the Weni Platform.

Credentials

The credentials used in the skills you define for your agent.

Description

Important information about your agent, where you can describe its purpose, capabilities, and other relevant details.

Instructions

Here you can define rules and guidelines that your agent should follow.

Guardrails

You can list boundaries and limitations for your agent, such as topics it should not discuss.

Name

The name of the skill that will be associated with the agent in the Weni Platform.

Source

The location or path where the skill can be found.

Description

Information about the skill, including its purpose and objectives.

Parameters

The parameters or variables used in your agent's skill.

docs.title: Basic Structure
docs.text:
The basic structure of your project should consist of your agent definition written in YAML and your agent's skills organized into directories. It is not mandatory to organize your skills in a 'skills' directory, but it is highly recommended as a best practice.

Based on the definition example below:

agent_definition.yaml
agents:
  sample_agent:
    name: "Sample Agent"
    description: "Weni's sample agent"
    instructions:
      - "You should always be polite, respectful and helpful, even if the user is not."
      - "If you don't know the answer, don't lie. Tell the user you don't know."
    guardrails:
      - "Don't talk about politics, religion or any other sensitive topic. Keep it neutral."
    skills:
      - get_order_status:
          name: "Get Order Status"
          source: 
            path: "skills/order_status"
            entrypoint: "lambda_function.lambda_handler"
          description: "Function to get the order status"
          parameters:
            - order_id:
                description: "Order ID"
                type: "string"
                required: true
      - get_order_details:
          name: "Get Order Details"                                                           
          source: 
            path: "skills/order_details"
            entrypoint: "lambda_function.lambda_handler"
          description: "Function to get the order details"
          parameters:
            - order_id:
                description: "Order ID"
                type: "string"
                required: true

Your project should have the following structure: 

your-project-name/
├── skills/
│   ├── get_orders_status/
│   ├── get_order_details/
└── agent_definition.yaml

docs.title: CEP Agent Example
docs.text: This example shows how to create an agent that can provide address information based on Brazilian postal codes (CEP).

docs.title: Agent Definition
docs.text:
Create a file named agents.yaml:

agents:
  sample_agent:
    name: "CEP Agent"
    description: "Weni's CEP agent"
    instructions:
      - "You are an expert in providing addresses to the user based on a postal code provided by the user"
      - "The user will send a ZIP code (postal code) and you must provide the address corresponding to this code."
    guardrails:
      - "Don't talk about politics, religion or any other sensitive topic. Keep it neutral."
    skills:
      - get_address:
          name: "Get Address"
          source: 
            path: "skills/cep_agent"
            entrypoint: "lambda_function.lambda_handler"
          description: "Function to get the address from the postal code"
          parameters:
            - cep:
                description: "postal code of a place"
                type: "string"
                required: true
                contact_field: true

docs.title: Lambda Function
docs.text:
Create a file skills/cep_agent/lambda_function.py:

import urllib.request

def cep_search(cep):
    headers = {
        'Content-Type': 'application/json',
        'Accept': 'application/json',
    }

    url = f'https://viacep.com.br/ws/{cep}/json/'

    req = urllib.request.Request(url, headers=headers)

    with urllib.request.urlopen(req) as response:
        response_data = response.read().decode('utf-8')

    return response_data


def lambda_handler(event, context):
    agent = event['agent']
    actionGroup = event['actionGroup']
    function = event['function']
    parameters = event.get('parameters', [])

    cep_value = None
    for param in parameters:
        if param['name'] == 'cep':
            cep_value = param['value']
            break

    response_body = {
        'TEXT': {
            'body': f"{cep_search(cep=cep_value)}"
        }
    }

    function_response = {
        'actionGroup': actionGroup,
        'function': function,
        'functionResponse': {
            'responseBody': response_body
        }
    }

    session_attributes = event.get('sessionAttributes', {})
    prompt_session_attributes = event.get('promptSessionAttributes', {})

    action_response = {
        'messageVersion': '1.0', 
        'response': function_response,
        'sessionAttributes': session_attributes,
        'promptSessionAttributes': prompt_session_attributes
    }

    return action_response

docs.title: Deployment Steps
docs.text:
1. Deploy the agent: 
    weni project push agents.yaml

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
After deployment, you can test the agent by:

  1. Opening your project in the Weni platform
  2. Finding the CEP Agent in your agents list
  3. Starting a conversation
  4. Sending a valid Brazilian postal code (e.g., "01311-000")

docs.title: Example Gallery
docs.text:
• CEP Agent

    Master the Weni CLI by building a high-performance agent capable of querying Brazilian postal codes.

    Getting started

docs.title: Authentication Guide
docs.text: Authentication is one of the most important steps to start developing your agents. To authenticate, you first need to have an account on the Weni platform. You can create your account here: Weni Platform.

docs.title: Authentication Process
docs.text:
Once you have your Weni account, it's time to authenticate using the CLI. Run the following command:

weni login

This command will open a browser window where you can login with your Weni account.

After logging in, you will be redirected to the CLI and you will be authenticated with a success message.

Login Successfully

Now you are authenticated and you can start using the Weni CLI with your projects.

docs.title: Installation Guide
docs.text: This guide will help you install the Weni CLI tool on your system. We provide two installation methods: a quick installation via pip and a manual installation using Poetry for development purposes.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python >= 3.12
  • Poetry >= 1.8.5

docs.title: Install via PIP
docs.text:
You can install the CLI directly using pip:

pip install weni-cli

docs.title: Manual Installation
docs.text:
1. Clone the repository: 

    git clone https://github.com/weni-ai/weni-cli.git
cd weni-cli

  2. Install dependencies and make the CLI executable: 

    poetry shell
poetry install

docs.title: Verifying Installation
docs.text:
After installation, verify that Weni CLI is properly installed:

weni --version

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
If you encounter any issues:

  1. Check our GitHub Issues
  2. Create a new issue with:
  3. Your operating system
  4. Python version (python --version)
  5. Error message
  6. Steps to reproduce

docs.title: Quick Start Guide
docs.text: This guide will help you get started with Weni CLI by creating your first agent. We'll create a simple CEP (Brazilian postal code) agent that can provide addresses based on postal codes.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
1. Install Weni CLI
  2. Have a Weni account

docs.title: 1. Login to Weni
docs.text:
weni login

This will open your browser for authentication. After successful login, you can close the browser tab.

docs.title: 2. List Your Projects
docs.text:
weni project list

This command will show all projects you have access to. Note down the UUID of the project you want to work with.

docs.title: 3. Select Your Project
docs.text:
weni project use your-project-uuid

Replace your-project-uuid with the UUID from the project list.

docs.title: 4. Verify Current Project
docs.text:
weni project current

This ensures you're working with the correct project.

docs.title: 5. Create Agent Definition
docs.text: Create a file named agents.yaml:

docs.title: Note that the path field in the skills section should point to the folder where the Lambda function for that skill is located.
docs.text:
agents:
  sample_agent:
    name: "CEP Agent"
    description: "Weni's CEP agent"
    instructions:
      - "You are an expert in providing addresses to the user based on a postal code provided by the user"
      - "The user will send a ZIP code (postal code) and you must provide the address corresponding to this code."
    guardrails:
      - "Don't talk about politics, religion or any other sensitive topic. Keep it neutral."
    skills:
      - get_address:
          name: "Get Address"
          source: 
            path: "skills/cep_agent"
            entrypoint: "lambda_function.lambda_handler"
          description: "Function to get the address from the postal code"
          parameters:
            - cep:
                description: "postal code of a place"
                type: "string"
                required: true

docs.title: 6. Create Agent skill folder
docs.text:
Create a folder named skills/cep_agent:

mkdir skills
mkdir skills/cep_agent
cd skills/cep_agent

docs.title: 7. Create Lambda Function inside skills/cep_agent folder
docs.text:
Create a file skills/cep_agent/lambda_function.py:

import urllib.request

def cep_search(cep):
    headers = {
        'Content-Type': 'application/json',
        'Accept': 'application/json',
    }
    url = f'https://viacep.com.br/ws/{cep}/json/'
    req = urllib.request.Request(url, headers=headers)
    with urllib.request.urlopen(req) as response:
        response_data = response.read().decode('utf-8')
    return response_data

def lambda_handler(event, context):
    parameters = event.get('parameters', [])
    cep_value = next((param['value'] for param in parameters 
                     if param['name'] == 'cep'), None)

    response_body = {'TEXT': {'body': cep_search(cep=cep_value)}}

    return {
        'messageVersion': '1.0',
        'response': {
            'actionGroup': event['actionGroup'],
            'function': event['function'],
            'functionResponse': {'responseBody': response_body}
        },
        'sessionAttributes': event.get('sessionAttributes', {}),
        'promptSessionAttributes': event.get('promptSessionAttributes', {})
    }

docs.title: 7.1. Create the requirements.txt file (optional)
docs.text:
If you are using any external libraries, you must create a requirements.txt file with the dependencies versions:

Example: 

urllib3==2.3.0

docs.title: 8. Deploy Agent
docs.text: weni project push agents.yaml

docs.title: What's Next?
docs.text:
• Learn more about agent configuration
  • Explore project management
  • See more examples

docs.title: Working with Agents
docs.text: Learn how to create, configure, and deploy AI agents using Weni CLI.

docs.title: Agent Definition File
docs.text:
Agents are defined using YAML files. Here's the basic structure:

agents:
  agent_id:
    name: "Agent Name"
    description: "Agent Description"
    instructions:
      - "Instruction 1"
      - "Instruction 2"
    guardrails:
      - "Guardrail 1"
    skills:
      - skill_name:
          name: "Skill Name"
          path: "path/to/agent_skill_folder"
          description: "Skill Description"
          parameters:
            - param_name:
                description: "Parameter Description"
                type: "string"
                required: true
                contact_field: true

docs.title: Key Components
docs.text:
1. Agent ID
  2. Unique identifier for your agent

  3. Used internally by the system

  4. Basic Information

  5. name: Display name (max 128 characters)

  6. description: Brief description of the agent's purpose

  7. Instructions

  8. Guide the agent's behavior
  9. Minimum 40 characters each

  10. Should be clear and specific

  11. Guardrails

  12. Define boundaries and limitations

  13. Prevent unwanted behavior

  14. Skills

  15. Custom functionalities
  16. Implemented as Lambda functions

docs.title: Lambda Function Structure
docs.text:
def lambda_handler(event, context):
    # Extract parameters
    parameters = event.get('parameters', [])

    # Process the request
    result = process_request(parameters)

    # Format response
    return {
        'messageVersion': '1.0',
        'response': {
            'actionGroup': event['actionGroup'],
            'function': event['function'],
            'functionResponse': {
                'responseBody': {
                    'TEXT': {'body': result}
                }
            }
        },
        'sessionAttributes': event.get('sessionAttributes', {}),
        'promptSessionAttributes': event.get('promptSessionAttributes', {})
    }

docs.title: Push Command
docs.text:
Deploy your agent using:

weni project push agents.yaml

The command: 1. Validates your YAML 2. Uploads skills 3. Creates/updates the agent

docs.title: Deployment Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Version Control
  2. Keep agent definitions in version control

  3. Document changes

  4. Testing

  5. Test locally when possible
  6. Start with staging environment

  7. Verify all skills work

  8. Organization

  9. Use clear file names
  10. Keep related files together
  11. Document dependencies

docs.title: Parameter Types
docs.text: Available parameter types: - string - number - boolean - array - object

docs.title: Response Formats
docs.text: Skills can return: - Text responses - Structured data - Error messages

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: Your skills should: 1. Validate inputs 2. Handle exceptions gracefully 3. Return meaningful error messages

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
1. Deployment Failures
  2. Check YAML syntax
  3. Verify skill paths

  4. Confirm project selection

  5. Skill Errors

  6. Verify skill entrypoint
  7. Test Lambda function locally
  8. Check parameter handling

  9. Verify API endpoints

  10. Agent Behavior

  11. Review instructions
  12. Check guardrails
  13. Test with various inputs

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Development Flow
  2. Develop locally
  3. Test in staging

  4. Deploy to production

  5. Monitoring

  6. Keep deployment logs
  7. Monitor skill performance

  8. Track user interactions

  9. Updates

  10. Plan changes carefully
  11. Test updates thoroughly
  12. Document modifications

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text: Learn how to authenticate with Weni CLI and manage your credentials.

docs.title: Login Process
docs.text:
Weni CLI uses OAuth2 for authentication. When you run the login command:

weni login

The following happens:

  1. A local web server starts on your machine
  2. Your default browser opens to the Weni login page
  3. After successful login, you're redirected back to the local server
  4. The CLI receives and stores your authentication token

docs.title: Token Storage
docs.text: Your authentication token is stored securely in your home directory. Never share or expose your authentication information.

docs.title: Token Refresh
docs.text:
Tokens are automatically refreshed when needed. You don't need to manually re-login unless:

  • You've logged out
  • Your token was revoked
  • You want to switch accounts

docs.title: Logout
docs.text:
To logout and remove your authentication:

  1. Delete your configuration file
  2. Run weni login again to authenticate with different credentials

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
1. Browser Doesn't Open
  2. Use the URL displayed in the terminal

  3. Check if you have a default browser configured

  4. Authentication Failures

  5. Verify your internet connection
  6. Check if your account has the necessary permissions

docs.title: Security Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Keep Your Token Safe
  2. Don't share your configuration file
  3. Use appropriate file permissions

  4. Don't expose the token in scripts or logs

  5. Regular Validation

  6. Periodically verify your authentication status
  7. Update your credentials if you suspect any security issues

docs.title: Project Management
docs.text: Learn how to manage your Weni projects using the CLI.

docs.title: List Projects
docs.text:
View all projects you have access to:

weni project list

This command shows: - Project UUID - Project name - Organization - Creation date

docs.title: Select Project
docs.text:
Set a project as your current working project:

weni project use <project-uuid>

Replace <project-uuid> with the UUID from the project list.

docs.title: View Current Project
docs.text:
Check which project you're currently working with:

weni project current

This shows: - Project UUID - Project name - Organization - Other relevant details

docs.title: Project Context
docs.text: The CLI maintains a "current project" context, which is used by other commands like project push. This context is stored in your .weni_cli file.

docs.title: Why Project Context Matters
docs.text:
• Simplifies command usage (no need to specify project in every command)
  • Prevents accidental deployments to wrong projects
  • Maintains consistent workflow

docs.title: Project Organization
docs.text:
1. Naming Conventions
  2. Use clear, descriptive project names

  3. Follow your organization's naming standards

  4. Project Structure

  5. Keep related agents together
  6. Maintain consistent file organization

docs.title: Working with Multiple Projects
docs.text:
1. Context Switching
  2. Always verify current project before operations

  3. Use project current to confirm active project

  4. Project Isolation

  5. Keep project-specific files in separate directories
  6. Use version control for project configurations

docs.title: Starting a New Project
docs.text:
1. Create project in Weni platform
  2. List projects to get UUID: 
    weni project list
  3. Select the new project: 
    weni project use <new-project-uuid>
  4. Verify selection: 
    weni project current

docs.title: Managing Multiple Projects
docs.text:
1. List available projects
  2. Switch between projects as needed
  3. Verify current project before operations
  4. Keep project files organized

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
1. Project Not Found
  2. Verify project UUID
  3. Check your access permissions

  4. Ensure you're in the correct environment

  5. Unable to Switch Projects

  6. Verify .weni_cli file permissions
  7. Check authentication status

  8. Confirm project exists and is accessible

  9. Project List Empty

  10. Verify authentication
  11. Check environment configuration
  12. Confirm you have project access

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text: If you encounter issues: 1. Check command syntax 2. Verify project access 3. Contact support if needed
